item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
The first image could not be sourced reliably and is assumed not compliant with CC 3.0. As such, it has since been removed.

The images at the bottom of the article are author generated, and will be under CC BY-SA 3.0, unless the author releases it under a different license.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I came over here from your Family Planning tale knowing nothing about the two brothers, and I gotta say, the skips themselves really live up to the mythology/folklore you made for them. It's intriguing to read about and terrifying to think about.

Kudos to you, take my +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: dr cuddles nominated for least cuddly doctor at the foundation

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Mister bubbles! Why won't you move?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If the symptoms end, maybe an amnesiac would help on the way to recovery, even if the symptoms of phobia remain? But no, I'm looking for happy endings here. Even if they were remanded to a public mental health facility with night lights, they're still highly contagious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe a Tale would be interesting at some point to explore that. I didn't think too much about when they grow up. I don't imagine any severe retardation problems. They would remain very afraid of the dark; possibly it would drive them insane if they survive for very long. The one that would survive long enough are the ones contained and treated by the Foundation, and I don't know how that would affect their mental development.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Works for me. Hm. I wonder if the afflicted adults suffer mental regression and arrested development? They retain a child-mind and can't mentally mature? I recognize very few people survive this, it would just be interesting to see how utterly screwed an adult victim of this would be?

Either way, I like it! I wanna see more of Mr Spiky. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wanted the entity to be different from people to people, but with a number of trademarks (it always has a single red eye and it's always called Mr. Something). That way the researchers will notice a connection between cases, while at the same time not being too sure about it's significance, because it's not always identical.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This specific science subject is in my area of expertise, so I was confident about that part. I was a bit more worried about mucking up the psychological or memetic aspect though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The only thing that furrows my brows here is how the entity seems to always be called a Mr. Something. I don't know it's just a bit iffy.

But this one is pretty good, I love the drawings, too. Very authentic. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You lost my interest at the bio-medical detail, just not a science I geek out over.
But then won it back double with the probably-memetic contagion vector.

It would be very easy when writing something like this to have the doctors jump to correct conclusion without sufficient support because authorial knowledge makes it obvious. But you succesfully navigated that pitfall, while at the same time not completly obscurign the important facts from the reader.

Very well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I added a bit at the very end of description about that. Essentially, patients remain affected and contagious. It's simply that it takes a children's innocence and belief to contract the disease, but once you have it it's permanent. My first idea was that adults could also contract the disease, but that would have complicated things without adding anything much to the story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a good start, but I need to see something more before I upvote: what happens to the children when they enter puberty or become adults?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought of using 'imaginary friend' instead of boogeyman, but then we are not sure how imaginary it is. I just could not find a good clinical word. Since I say it's something they belive in rather then an actual observed phenomenon, I guessed it was OK to use a non-formal word for that. If I come up with a better word I will change it. For the Bright/burning, I used quotation marks because those are always the words used by the children to describe it (so the quotation marks are really quotations marks here).

The last picture was in fact made with a large felt-tip pen, even though it does look like a Paint drawing (I played with the contrast and tones after scanning them).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thoroughly enjoyed this article, but I wasn't a fan of the layman's/direct quotes to describe the entity, like "boogeyman" and "bright"/"burning". I feel like you could still describe those clinically without taking away the intriguing aspects of the entity, but that may just be me. Regardless, you took a cool concept and went further with it, and your execution of it is excellent.

I kind of wish you'd tried to make the children's interviews more child-like than transcript, though - it probably would have made it seem more innocent, which always adds on a level of additional shock for me. Your last picture also kind of looks a little too digitally made. But these are really just nitpicks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What I was trying to say here is that they recognize the importance of this ''Misterr'' as something probably supernatural. However, they don't know if it's a real entity, like a demon, or simply a memetic danger. I might rewrite that sentence a bit better to make it more clear.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
when i read all the mr.something i thought: what? wondertainment? this?
i really like this, but after these "coincides" i cant believe the foundation doesnt see a connection. like

They are also convinced of the existence of a ''boogeyman'' which they invariably describe as a black figure with a single ''bright'' or ''burning'' eye. It is unclear at present whether this persona is a real entity or a mental component of the disease.

would maybe be

They are also convinced of the existence of a ''boogeyman'' which they invariably describe as a black figure with a single ''bright'' or ''burning'' eye. It is unclear at present whether this persona is a real entity or a mental component of the disease, though it seems highly possible.

have my upvote anyway

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks to everyone for reading it! I'm glad to see all the good comments. I should have mentioned it earlier, but I would like to thank DrSevere, VAElynx and DrBerggren who gave feedback on the draft.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Have a well deserved upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, I made them myself. It's inspired by the thousands of shadow monsters I've seen since my childhood.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
He did I think - besides, those are pretty generic kid-drawn monster likes. I drew *fucktons* of stuff like that when I was about 5-6.

Amusing story - I doodled a bunch of toothy germs vaguely inspired by the There Once was a Life series, except nastier and toothier that the doctor actually used to creep some other, younger kids into compliance XD. I guess, no wonder I ended up writing creepypasta.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dr. Cuddles, did you make the drawings yourself? The art reminds me of Bogleech's work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mighty good stuff. You got me shaking in my boots. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good show!

Like it that it's not just a weird skin disease, or a weird monster, or something that screws with your brain, but a fine mixture!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really good. Those drafts made me feel the fear for dark. Original, well written, that hook hit my face really hard. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very nice. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The picture on the far right makes me think of Dr. Gears' proposal for SCP-001.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This. +1 regardless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well done. Not only do I like it, I like it enough to tell you so.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Told you I'd upvote in the draft thread.
This is good and you should feel good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
People who read my two SCPs will think I have something against children, but it's actually the opposite. It is said we should figure out what scares us the most and I realized while writing this that for me it's bad thing happening to kids. Books about children, like Black House or Gone, Baby Gone are always more horrifying to me.

At first I thought about the disease affecting everyone; possibly from a The Factory Sun Lotion, but then I developed the phobia aspect and it just grabbed me more like that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, when I read it I immediately thought "reverse Xeroderma". But the way you explored it, and especially those pictures at the end, really made it work for me. I do agree that the interview transcripts need some work, but it's not enough to prevent an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cuddles, broski, you really don't live up to your namesake. This is pretty rad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you. Yes, I tried to make texts and pictures that looked like they came from children, but I found it hard to do that convincingly. Actually, the notes are transcripts of interviews, not directly written by them.

Also, some background and insights:

The biological aspect is based on the rare disease Xeroderma pigmentosum. These patients have an extreme sensitivity to sunlight because their cells cannot repair DNA damage caused by UV. I imagined a coherent way to cause the reverse effect, to create people that would ''burn'' in the dark.

It's normal to have some level of fear of the dark, especially for kids, but in these modern times we have electricity and people are less supersticious. So darkness does not hold as much fear as it once did. We know there's no monster in the dark; we have a better understanding of nature and new fears that come with it.

The boogeyman represents the fear of the dark (not darkness itself). It's not too happy about losing it's customers and decides to find new ways to keep people afraid. It could be a real antropomophic personification with a mind of it's own or maybe it's just some subconscious part of the mind of these kids. But at some level, the kids are doing this to themselves. Fear is something that eats at you from within. The boogeyman is a part of you; it can't create real monsters in the dark, but it can turn you own body against yourself.

Keeping the patients in the light maintains them alive longer but it does not release them from the constant terror of the dark. In the last sentence I summarize the whole despair of this: the fear that even death would not be a release from suffering.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, but the notes don't feel like they were written by children.
